subsystem: PHP `phar` (PHP Archive) extension â€” the signature-verification routine `phar_verify_signature()`. Given an open stream over a .phar archive, it recomputes a hash/OpenSSL signature over the first `end_of_phar` bytes of the archive and compares it against the signature bytes (`sig`/`sig_len`) that were parsed out of the archive manifest, returning SUCCESS only if they match. It is the integrity gate that decides whether an untrusted archive is accepted.
provenance: Identified by the function name `phar_verify_signature` and the PHAR_SIG_* signature-type constants (PHAR_SIG_OPENSSL, PHAR_SIG_SHA512/256/SHA1/MD5), the `PHAR_HAVE_OPENSSL` / `PHAR_HASH_OK` feature macros, and the surrounding PHP/Zend engine API (`php_stream_*`, `zend_string`, `ZSTR_VAL/ZSTR_LEN`, `spprintf`, `efree`, `phar_hex_str`, `phar_call_openssl_signverify`, `EVP_*` from OpenSSL). This is `ext/phar/util.c` from the PHP interpreter. The sample header `/* sample: primevul_c_train_3078_vuln */` also names it as a phar sample.
trust_boundary: The .phar archive is fully attacker-controlled: a PHP application that opens a phar (directly, via `phar://` stream wrapper, or through auto-loading) hands this routine values that were parsed out of the archive. `sig_type` selects which case runs, and â€” crucially â€” the signature bytes `sig` and their length `sig_len` are read from the archive's trailer, as is `end_of_phar` (the claimed size of the signed region). All three are hostile. The only trusted input on the OpenSSL path is the local `fname.pubkey` file. The invariant a safe implementation must uphold is that the attacker-supplied `sig_len` is validated against the fixed digest size BEFORE `sig` is read, and that `sig_len` is never treated as a signed quantity that can go negative.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point. `fp` is the open stream over the archive; `end_of_phar` (a `size_t`) is the number of leading bytes of the archive that were signed; `sig_type` selects the algorithm; `sig`/`sig_len` are the signature bytes and their length as parsed from the archive; `fname` is the archive path (used to derive the public-key filename); `signature`/`signature_len` are OUT parameters that receive a freshly hex-encoded copy of the signature; `error` receives an allocated error string. Locals: `read_size` and `len` are `int` chunk counters, `read_len` is a signed 64-bit `zend_off_t` remaining-byte counter, and `buf[1024]` is the fixed read buffer that the archive body is streamed through into the hash/verify context. `sig_len` being a signed `int` (rather than an unsigned `size_t`) is the first thing to note: it is attacker-influenced and is later handed to length-sensitive primitives.

The archive stream is rewound to the start so hashing begins at byte 0, then control dispatches on the attacker-chosen `sig_type`. Because `sig_type` is taken from the archive, the attacker fully decides which of the branches below executes; a hostile archive can select the algorithm whose fixed digest size differs most from the `sig_len` it also supplies.

The OpenSSL public-key signature branch. Two build variants exist: when `PHAR_HAVE_OPENSSL` is defined the extension links OpenSSL directly and declares a `BIO`, `EVP_PKEY`, an `EVP_MD` set to SHA-1, and an `EVP_MD_CTX`; otherwise it will shell the work out to a separate `openssl` module and only needs a `tempsig` scratch int. In the non-linked variant it first checks that the `openssl` module is actually registered, emitting "openssl not loaded" and failing if not. `pubkey`, `pfile`, and `pfp` hold the public-key string, its filename, and its stream.

The public key is loaded from a sidecar file named `<fname>.pubkey`: the path is formatted with `spprintf`, opened read-binary, and its whole contents slurped into the `pubkey` zend_string via `php_stream_copy_to_mem(... PHP_STREAM_COPY_ALL ...)`. If the file cannot be opened, cannot be read, or is empty (`!ZSTR_LEN(pubkey)`), the routine closes any open stream, sets an error, and fails. Otherwise the stream is closed and `pubkey` holds the trusted verification key. Note the short-circuit `||` guarantees `pubkey` is only assigned when `pfp` is non-NULL, and only dereferenced by `ZSTR_LEN` after a successful copy.

The non-linked build delegates the actual crypto to `phar_call_openssl_signverify()`, passing the stream, the signed-region length `end_of_phar`, the public key bytes/length, and `&sig`/`&tempsig`. `tempsig` is seeded from the attacker-controlled `sig_len`; after a successful verify, `sig_len` is overwritten with whatever `tempsig` now holds. On failure the pubkey is released and the routine fails with an error. The signature length flows into an external verifier as a signed int here.

The OpenSSL-linked variant wraps the pubkey bytes in a memory BIO, then parses a PEM public key from it with `PEM_read_bio_PUBKEY`. The BIO is freed and the pubkey string released immediately after parsing. If either the BIO allocation or the key parse fails, it emits "openssl signature could not be processed" and fails. On success `key` is a usable `EVP_PKEY`.

The verify context is initialised for SHA-1. `read_len` (signed 64-bit) is set to the attacker-claimed signed-region size `end_of_phar`. `read_size` is clamped to the 1024-byte buffer: if `read_len > sizeof(buf)` it starts at 1024, else it is the (truncated-to-int) remaining length. The stream is seeked to 0 and the body is streamed in â‰¤1024-byte chunks; each chunk feeds `EVP_VerifyUpdate`, `read_len` is decremented by the bytes actually read, and once fewer than `read_size` bytes remain, `read_size` is lowered so the final read does not overshoot the signed region. The loop is guarded by `read_size &&`, so it stops cleanly if the remaining size ever reaches zero. This bounds hashing to at most `end_of_phar` bytes.

The accumulated digest is verified against the attacker-supplied `sig`/`sig_len` with `EVP_VerifyFinal`, which returns 1 only on a valid signature; anything else cleans up the context, sets "broken openssl signature", and fails. On success the context is cleaned up and â€” for BOTH build variants â€” the raw signature is hex-encoded via `phar_hex_str(sig, sig_len, signature)`, with the encoded length stored through `*signature_len`. Note that `sig_len` (a signed int) is passed directly as the signature-length argument to `EVP_VerifyFinal` and to `phar_hex_str`, so the length OpenSSL reads from `sig` is attacker-chosen.

The SHA-512 branch (only compiled when `PHAR_HASH_OK`). A fixed 64-byte `digest` buffer and a SHA-512 context are set up, `read_len` is seeded from `end_of_phar`, and the same chunked read loop hashes the signed region. `PHP_SHA512Final` writes the 64-byte digest. The computed digest is then compared to the archive-supplied `sig` with `memcmp(digest, sig, sizeof(digest))` â€” reading a FIXED 64 bytes from `sig` regardless of `sig_len`. On mismatch it fails with "broken signature"; on match the digest is hex-encoded and returned. Unlike the OpenSSL loop, this loop lacks the leading `read_size &&` guard.

The SHA-256 branch, structurally identical to SHA-512 but with a 32-byte `digest`. It hashes the signed region and compares the computed 32-byte digest to the archive `sig` with `memcmp(digest, sig, sizeof(digest))`, again reading a fixed 32 bytes from `sig` with no reference to `sig_len`. Success path hex-encodes and returns the digest.

When the hashing algorithms are not compiled in (`PHAR_HASH_OK` undefined), SHA-512 and SHA-256 archives are rejected as "unsupported signature". This is the safe fall-through for builds lacking those hashes.

The SHA-1 branch with a 20-byte `digest`. Same hashing loop and the same fixed-size comparison `memcmp(digest, sig, sizeof(digest))` reading 20 bytes from `sig`. This case is always compiled (not gated by `PHAR_HASH_OK`).

The MD5 branch with a 16-byte `digest`, otherwise identical: hash the signed region, then `memcmp(digest, sig, sizeof(digest))` reads a fixed 16 bytes from the archive-supplied `sig`. Success hex-encodes the digest and returns it.

Any unknown `sig_type` is rejected as "broken or unsupported signature". If a case fell through to here without returning FAILURE, the function returns SUCCESS, signalling that the archive's signature is valid and the caller may trust and load it.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Out-of-bounds READ of the attacker-controlled signature buffer. In every hash branch the comparison reads a FIXED number of bytes from `sig` â€” `sizeof(digest)` = 64 (SHA-512, line 150), 32 (SHA-256, line 183), 20 (SHA-1, line 224), 16 (MD5, line 257) â€” but `sig` was allocated/parsed from the archive trailer with only `sig_len` bytes, and `sig_len` is NEVER checked against `sizeof(digest)` before the compare. An attacker crafts a phar whose manifest declares e.g. SHA-512 (`sig_type`) yet supplies a signature of only 1 byte (`sig_len == 1`); `memcmp` then reads 63 bytes past the end of the `sig` allocation. Impact: heap over-read â†’ crash / denial of service, and potentially disclosure of adjacent heap contents through timing or side effects, weakening the integrity gate. The invariant the implementation MUST uphold is: reject the archive (return FAILURE) unless `sig_len == sizeof(digest)` for the selected algorithm BEFORE calling `memcmp`.

The OpenSSL branch passes the attacker-controlled `sig` together with the signed `int sig_len` straight into `EVP_VerifyFinal`, whose length parameter is an `unsigned int`. `sig_len` originates from the archive and is only ever assigned from `tempsig` on the non-linked path; nothing here bounds it against the true size of the `sig` allocation or rejects a negative value. If `sig_len` is negative it sign-extends to a huge unsigned length, and if it is simply larger than the real buffer, OpenSSL reads `sig_len` bytes from `sig`, causing an out-of-bounds read. Impact: crash / possible heap disclosure inside the crypto verify. Invariant: `sig_len` must be non-negative and must not exceed the number of bytes actually present in `sig` (and should match the DER signature length) before it is handed to `EVP_VerifyFinal`/`phar_hex_str`.

After a successful OpenSSL verify, the raw `sig`/`sig_len` are hex-encoded by `phar_hex_str`, which reads `sig_len` bytes from `sig`. The same unbounded, signed `sig_len` that reaches `EVP_VerifyFinal` reaches this call, so an oversized or negative `sig_len` produces an out-of-bounds read while formatting the signature for display, and an allocation sized from a bogus length. Invariant: `sig_len` must be validated (non-negative, â‰¤ buffer size) before it is used as a read length anywhere in this function.

Signedness / truncation of the read counters. `read_size` and `len` are `int`, while `read_len` is a signed 64-bit `zend_off_t` seeded from the `size_t end_of_phar`. In the clamp `read_size = (int)read_len` (lines 95/137/170/211/244) and in `read_size = (int)read_len` inside each loop (lines 105/144/177/218/251), a `read_len` value above INT_MAX is truncated. Because the branch only casts when `read_len <= sizeof(buf)` (1024) the initial clamp is safe, but the general reliance on `int` for stream sizes is fragile; more importantly the comparison `read_len > sizeof(buf)` mixes a signed `zend_off_t` with an unsigned `size_t`, forcing signed-to-unsigned promotion. These are correctness/robustness hazards rather than a direct overflow here, but they are worth confirming against very large `end_of_phar` values from a hostile archive.

Inconsistent loop guard between branches. The OpenSSL loop (line 100) is written `while (read_size && (len = php_stream_read(...)) > 0)`, but the four hash loops (lines 140/173/214/247) omit the leading `read_size &&` test. When `end_of_phar` is 0 (or once the remaining length reaches 0), these loops call `php_stream_read(fp, buf, read_size)` with `read_size == 0`. In practice a zero-length read returns 0 and the loop terminates, so this is not a memory-safety bug, but the divergence signals the two code paths were not kept in sync and should be confirmed to behave identically for empty / fully-consumed archives (and that `read_size` can never go negative and be passed as a size to `php_stream_read`).

summary: This is PHP phar's signature-verification gate. Its central flaw is that the attacker-controlled signature length (`sig_len`, a signed int parsed from the archive) is never validated against the fixed digest size: every hash branch does `memcmp(digest, sig, sizeof(digest))` and the OpenSSL branch passes `sig`/`sig_len` to `EVP_VerifyFinal`/`phar_hex_str`, so a crafted phar with a short (or negative-length) signature triggers an out-of-bounds read of the heap-allocated `sig` buffer â€” a remotely-reachable crash / info-leak in the very code meant to decide whether an untrusted archive is trustworthy.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: phar_verify_signature(...) signature â€” params fp, end_of_phar (size_t), sig_type (php_uint32), sig (char*), sig_len (int), fname, signature (char**), signature_len (int*), error (char**)
reviewed_anchors.reason: sig/sig_len are attacker-controlled and drive unbounded reads; sig_len is a signed int
Fixed read scratch buffer; reads into it are bounded by read_size which is clamped to sizeof(buf), no overflow of buf itself
int/size_t/zend_off_t mixing and (int) truncation of stream sizes â€” robustness/signedness hazard
Bounds how many bytes are hashed; loop decrements correctly and is capped at end_of_phar, no OOB on buf, but it is attacker-controlled input feeding read_len
Fixed digest sizes are the read length in the memcmp against the shorter attacker sig buffer
Reads sizeof(digest) bytes from sig without checking sig_len â€” out-of-bounds read
Signed sig_len passed as OpenSSL length; negative/oversized value causes OOB read
Encodes sig_len bytes from sig; inherits the unbounded/signed sig_len read
Reads exactly sizeof(digest) from the local digest buffer â€” bounds are correct here
External verifier; tempsig seeded from sig_len â€” latent risk lives in that implementation (not shown), noted under the sig_len findings
Inconsistent guard between OpenSSL and hash loops; confirm no negative/zero read_size hazard

reviewed_anchors.anchor: if (read_len > sizeof(buf)) { read_size = sizeof(buf); } else { read_size = (int)read_len; } (lines 92-96 etc.)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Clamps read_size to buffer size; cast is only taken when read_len<=1024 so no truncation there â€” covered under the signedness finding

reviewed_anchors.anchor: PHAR_SIG_OPENSSL / PHAR_SIG_SHA512 / PHAR_SIG_SHA256 / PHAR_SIG_SHA1 / PHAR_SIG_MD5 constants
reviewed_anchors.reason: Enum selectors; switch has a default FAILURE and #else unsupported handling, so unknown/uncompiled types are rejected safely
Build-time selectors; both compile-time variants of each branch were reviewed

reviewed_anchors.anchor: zend_string *pubkey load via php_stream_copy_to_mem / released on all paths (lines 20,36,52,63,71,80)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Pubkey lifetime is handled: released on every failure and success path in the OpenSSL branch; no leak/UAF observed in the shown code

reviewed_anchors.anchor: spprintf(&pfile, ...) / efree(pfile) and error spprintf calls
reviewed_anchors.reason: Allocation freed immediately; error strings only written when error!=NULL â€” no NULL-deref or leak
Only reached when a case breaks without returning FAILURE; correct control flow

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read of archive signature in fixed-size digest comparison
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (fixed-size compare against a shorter attacker-sized buffer)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0018_cwe119.c (and the caller in ext/phar/util.c that parses sig/sig_len from the manifest â€” file not provided)
findings.invariant: For each hash algorithm the implementation must reject the archive unless the parsed signature length equals the fixed digest size, i.e. `sig_len == sizeof(digest)`, BEFORE performing the memcmp; the number of bytes read from `sig` must never exceed `sig_len`.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted phar declares a hash `sig_type` (e.g. PHAR_SIG_SHA512) but supplies a signature shorter than the digest (e.g. sig_len==1). `memcmp` reads a fixed sizeof(digest) bytes (64/32/20/16) from the `sig` heap allocation, reading past its end. Result: heap buffer over-read â†’ crash / DoS and potential adjacent-heap information exposure, in the routine that gates whether an untrusted archive is accepted.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm in the caller (phar_parse_pharfile) whether `sig` is allocated with exactly `sig_len` bytes and whether `sig_len` is validated to equal the digest size for the chosen algorithm before phar_verify_signature runs; if not, the over-read is reachable.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled signed sig_len passed as OpenSSL signature length
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via unvalidated / signed length
findings.invariant: `sig_len` must be non-negative and must not exceed the number of bytes actually present in the `sig` allocation before it is used as the length argument to EVP_VerifyFinal.
findings.failure_mode: `sig_len` is a signed int taken from the archive; nothing bounds it against the real size of `sig`. EVP_VerifyFinal takes an unsigned length, so a negative sig_len sign-extends to a huge value and an oversized value simply over-reads `sig` â€” an out-of-bounds read inside the crypto verify, causing a crash or heap disclosure.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify how `sig_len` is derived and bounded in the caller; confirm whether a negative or oversized value can reach this call for a hostile archive.

findings.title: Unbounded sig_len re-used when hex-encoding the verified signature
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0018_cwe119.c (phar_hex_str body â€” file not provided)
findings.invariant: `sig_len` must be validated (non-negative, â‰¤ size of `sig`) before being used as the read length for hex encoding.
findings.failure_mode: After a successful OpenSSL verify, phar_hex_str reads `sig_len` bytes from `sig` to build the hex string. The same unbounded/signed sig_len that reaches EVP_VerifyFinal reaches here, producing an out-of-bounds read while formatting and an allocation sized from a bogus length.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect phar_hex_str to confirm it reads exactly `sig_len` bytes and does not itself re-validate, so the over-read propagates.

findings.title: Signed int / size_t mixing in stream-length counters
findings.anchor: int read_size, len; zend_off_t read_len; read_size = (int)read_len
findings.invariant: Stream sizes derived from the untrusted `end_of_phar` must be carried in types wide/unsigned enough that clamping and comparison never truncate or misbehave; `read_size` must never become negative when passed to php_stream_read.
findings.failure_mode: `read_len` (signed 64-bit) seeded from `size_t end_of_phar` is compared to `sizeof(buf)` (unsigned) and later cast to `int read_size`. The initial clamp is safe because the cast only runs when read_len<=1024, but the general int-based accounting is fragile for very large or crafted sizes; worth confirming that read_size can never be passed negative to php_stream_read.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm read_len can never be negative entering the loop and that read_size stays in [0, sizeof(buf)] for all attacker-chosen end_of_phar.

findings.title: Divergent loop guard between OpenSSL and hash read loops
findings.invariant: All read loops should treat a zero/exhausted `read_size` identically and never pass a negative size to php_stream_read.
findings.failure_mode: The OpenSSL loop guards with `read_size &&` (line 100); the four hash loops do not. For an empty archive (end_of_phar==0) the hash loops call php_stream_read with read_size==0. A zero-length read returns 0 so the loop exits â€” not a memory-safety bug, but the divergence indicates the paths were not kept in sync and should be verified to behave identically.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm php_stream_read with size 0 (and any transient read_size==0) returns 0 and cannot loop or be interpreted as an error path differently across the branches.

audit_checklist: In the caller (ext/phar/util.c phar_parse_pharfile), confirm whether `sig` is allocated with exactly `sig_len` bytes and whether `sig_len` is validated to equal the algorithm's digest size (64/32/20/16) BEFORE phar_verify_signature â€” the fix for the memcmp over-read.
Add/verify an explicit `if (sig_len != sizeof(digest)) return FAILURE;` in each hash branch before memcmp.
Confirm `sig_len` cannot be negative or larger than the `sig` allocation before it reaches EVP_VerifyFinal (line 109) and phar_hex_str (line 123).
Trace how `end_of_phar` is derived from the archive and confirm read_len/read_size accounting cannot truncate or produce a negative read_size for large crafted sizes.
Verify phar_hex_str reads exactly its length argument and performs no independent bounds check that would mask an oversized sig_len.
Confirm the pubkey zend_string is released on every OpenSSL exit path and that php_stream_read with size 0 is benign across all loops.
